summary: Drew DeVault explains the design philosophy behind Sourcehut's intentionally minimal, brutalist web interface. The core principle is that Sourcehut is an engineering tool, so the design prioritizes bringing relevant content to the forefront while minimizing distractions. He contrasts Sourcehut's approach with GitHub and GitLab, criticizing their attention-grabbing navbars, marketing elements, and visual clutter. Colors are used sparingly and purposefully—blue for primary actions, red for dangerous ones—and icons are almost never shown without accompanying text. DeVault acknowledges he's not a skilled UI designer, arguing that simplicity makes good design attainable even for engineer-oriented developers.
key_points: Sourcehut's design treats every page as an engineering tool, ensuring the content you came for is the first thing that grabs your attention
GitHub and GitLab are criticized for cluttering their interfaces with marketing material, sign-up boxes, and visually distracting elements unrelated to the user's purpose
Color is used as a deliberate signaling mechanism: blue for primary actions, red for dangerous ones, gray for navigation that shouldn't distract
Icons are almost never used without accompanying text, rejecting the trend of icon-heavy interfaces that require users to guess meaning
The simplicity of the design system makes it easy for non-designers to create consistent, functional pages
money_quotes: sr.ht is an engineering tool first and foremost, and when you're there it's probably because you're in engineering mode.
The first thing on any page to grab your attention should be the thing that brought you there.
The nav can take you away from this page, but it's colored a light grey to avoid being distracting and each link is another engineering tool - no marketing material or fluff.
If you're logged out, you get a giant sign-up box which pushes the content halfway off the page.
Each button also is unambiguous and relies on the text within itself rather than the text nearby.
Interactive icons (things you are expected to click) are never shown without text that clarifies what happens when you click them.
Assigning an icon to every action or detail is not necessary and would add more distractions to the screen.
I'm not a particularly skilled UI designer, so keeping it simple like this also helps to make a reasonably nice UI attainable for an engineer-oriented developer like me.
key_insight: Good tool design means ruthlessly eliminating visual noise so the interface disappears and the user's actual work takes center stage.
